one_line_takeaway: MultiBreak scales multi-turn jailbreak benchmarking to 10,389 prompts across 2,665 harmful intents via an active-learning loop of generator fine-tuning + uncertainty-guided rewriting, achieving up to 54% higher ASR@1 than the strongest prior multi-turn dataset (MHJ) and revealing that some safety categories deemed low-risk in single-turn settings are highly exploitable in multi-turn conversation.
key_finding: MultiBreak achieves substantially higher ASR than all four baseline multi-turn datasets across nearly all victim/judge combinations. At ASR@1 under LLaMA Guard judging, MultiBreak scores 0.833 (DeepSeek-7B), 0.811 (Qwen3-8B), 0.682 (LLaMA3.1-8B), 0.677 (Gemini-2.5-FL), and 0.748 (GPT-4.1-mini) -- versus the strongest baseline MHJ's 0.293, 0.437, 0.488, 0.401, 0.402 respectively on the same victims. The paper reports this as up to a 54.0% higher ASR on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and 34.6% higher on GPT-4.1-mini than the second-best dataset (MHJ) at ASR@1. At ASR@10, MultiBreak exceeds 88% (LLaMA Guard) and 78% (GPT-4o-mini) across all five victim models. The dataset's diversity score (Eq.6, App. A.4.1) is 0.942, the highest among compared datasets (CoSafe 0.843, MHJ 0.810, SafeDialBench 0.762, RedQueen 0.680). Fine-grained category analysis (ASR@1 across top-5/bottom-5 categories, aggregated over five victims) shows categories such as high-risk medical/financial advice, cybercrime intrusion, phishing/credential theft, and child abuse reach ASR >= 79%, while AI-interaction-risk/manipulation categories are as low as 60% ASR -- indicating uneven, category-dependent LLM vulnerability that single-turn or template-limited benchmarks miss. Notably, all attack-generator fine-tuning used only open-source models (LLaMA3-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-14B), yet the resulting benchmark still achieves competitive ASR against closed-source victims (GPT-4.1-mini, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite), which the authors highlight as evidence that vulnerabilities induced by open-source-generated attacks transfer to closed-source models.
core_contribution: MultiBreak, a scalable and diverse multi-turn jailbreak benchmark of 10,389 adversarial prompts spanning 2,665 unique harmful intents (9 coarse / 26 fine-grained safety categories), built via a pool-based active-learning pipeline that iteratively fine-tunes attack-generator LLMs (LLaMA3-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with full SFT, DeepSeek-Distill-Qwen-14B with LoRA) and applies uncertainty-guided rewriting to convert borderline attack samples into reliable jailbreaks. The paper's own contribution is the dataset-construction framework (data diversification + active-learning loop with a composite ASR/uncertainty/faithfulness acquisition function + uncertainty-guided rewriter) and the resulting benchmark itself, not the underlying multi-turn jailbreak techniques (e.g. Crescendo, MRJ-Agent) which are cited only as related prior attack strategies.
threat_model: Attacker A transforms a harmful intent q into a sequence of multi-turn adversarial prompts q_adv = (q_adv^1,...,q_adv^n), n drawn Uniform(2,6), aimed at a victim LLM V (open- or closed-source, safety-aligned) to elicit a response r=V(q_adv) that violates safety policy, as determined by a judge J(q_adv, V(q_adv)) in {0,1}. The attacker's generation process itself is pre-scripted (not adaptively interactive with victim responses turn-by-turn), and success is measured via attack success rate (ASR) across multiple victim models V and multiple judges J to avoid single-model/single-judge bias. The paper frames this as a benchmark-construction problem (pool-based active learning over harmful intents) rather than proposing a new jailbreak algorithm against a specific deployed system.
limitations: The pdf_full_text extraction covered the main body (Sections 1-4.3, roughly the first ~22 pages / up to Table 2's ASR@10 rows); appendices beyond that point (containing further ablations, the diversity-score formula in full, the ASR@10 full table, defense evaluations against X-Boundary and NBF-LLM, and any explicit Limitations/Ethics/Broader-Impact section) were not extracted and are not reflected here. Within the paper: the paper itself acknowledges the attack prompts are pre-scripted rather than generated interactively in response to victim replies, which could reduce realism relative to a fully adaptive multi-turn attacker; it argues (but the underlying quantitative coherence analysis lives in an unextracted appendix, A.2.5) that conversational coherence is nonetheless maintained. The paper also notes ASR varies substantially by judge (LLaMA Guard vs. GPT-4o-mini disagree considerably per victim/dataset, e.g. MHJ scores higher under LLaMA Guard while CoSafe/SafeDialBench score higher under GPT-4o-mini), which is a known judge-bias problem the paper mitigates via multi-judge evaluation during construction but which still leaves residual disagreement in the final reported numbers. Naive fine-tuning without the active-learning/rewrite loop caps ASR around 25% (LLaMA3-8B-Instruct), underscoring that the reported gains substantially depend on the iterative acquisition-and-rewrite process rather than fine-tuning alone.

datasets_used: MultiBreak (this paper's own benchmark: 10,389 multi-turn adversarial prompts, 2,665 unique harmful intents, 9 coarse / 26 fine-grained categories)
CoSafe (Yu et al., 2024) -- baseline, 1,400 samples, 961 unique intents
MHJ (Li et al., 2024b) -- baseline, 537 samples, 406 unique intents
SafeDialBench (Cao et al., 2025), English subset -- baseline, 2,037 samples, 1,078 unique intents
RedQueen (Jiang et al., 2024) -- baseline, 1,400 x 40 templates, 656 unique intents

Five source multi-turn datasets and nine source single-turn intent datasets used to build the initial pool (Bhardwaj & Poria 2023; Yu et al. 2024; Cao et al. 2025; Li et al. 2024b; Jiang et al. 2024; plus nine single-turn sources listed in Appendix A.9)
quantitative_results: Diversity score (Eq.6): MultiBreak 0.942 vs CoSafe 0.843, MHJ 0.810, SafeDialBench 0.762, RedQueen 0.680
Initial D0 ASR on LLaMA3.1-8B-Instruct (LLaMA Guard judge): 10.77%, i.e. +4.47% over CoSafe and +3.77% over RedQueen
Naive fine-tuning of LLaMA3-8B-Instruct without active learning: ASR only 25%
Prompting vs fine-tuning on Mistral-7B-Instruct: prompting yields 2% ASR, fine-tuning raises it to 25%
ASR@1, LLaMA Guard judge -- MultiBreak: DeepSeek-7B 0.833, Qwen3-8B 0.811, LLaMA3.1-8B 0.682, Gemini-2.5-FL 0.677, GPT-4.1-mini 0.748
ASR@1, LLaMA Guard judge -- MHJ (best baseline): DeepSeek-7B 0.293, Qwen3-8B 0.437, LLaMA3.1-8B 0.488, Gemini-2.5-FL 0.401, GPT-4.1-mini 0.402
Reported headline gap: up to 54.0% higher ASR on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B and 34.6% higher on GPT-4.1-mini vs. second-best dataset at ASR@1
ASR@5, LLaMA Guard judge -- MultiBreak: DeepSeek-7B 0.957, Qwen3-8B 0.934, LLaMA3.1-8B 0.913, Gemini-2.5-FL 0.851, GPT-4.1-mini 0.857
ASR@10: MultiBreak exceeds 88% (LLaMA Guard) and 78% (GPT-4o-mini) across all five victim models

Fine-grained category ASR@1 (aggregated across five victims): top categories (high-risk medical/financial advice, cybercrime intrusion, phishing/credential theft, child abuse) >= 79%; bottom categories (AI-interaction risks/manipulation) as low as 60%
